Comparable bold declarations are the sixth century inscriptions on the mountains in Shandong. Here one finds the same seven characters, , Mount Yin 2. the reduced five character group, bore boluomi 般若波羅蜜, , Mount Culai, Mount Tao. or simply the two characters, bore 般若. , Mount Yi 2. Such succinct character groups function as invocations. They represent the very Perfection of Wisdom, making it visible and tangible, just as an engraved Buddha name conjures up the presence of that very Buddha. At the same time, these few characters, as with the titles in Jingtai’s Catalog, also stand for an entire sutra. They can be understood as the essence of the Prajñāpāramitā literature in its most condensed form.See .

We do not know who made the decision to stop the engraving of Monk Jingtai’s catalog in its tracks with this invocation of the Great Perfection of Wisdom. Apparently, several groups of monks, probably with different, possibly also competing, affiliations were active at the Grove.

After the exclamatory column 69, the engravings continue from columns 70 to 119 with the second part of the chapter “Bodhisattva High and Noble Virtuous King” (“Gaogui dewang pusa pin” 高貴德王菩薩品) from the Nirvana Sutra. The printed Taishō version gives a slightly longer title for this chapter, “Chapter on the Bodhisattva High and Noble Virtuous King, whose Radiating Light Illuminates All” (“Guangming bianzhao gaogui dewang pusa pin” 光明遍照高貴德王菩薩品). In the Taishō , the chapter is number 10 in the sutra, which has a total of 13 chapters. This tenth chapter has six parts, running from scroll 21 to 26. The engraved part is the entire scroll 22 in the Taishō . No subsequent scroll from the Sutra was engraved at the Grove. On the wall, however, this scroll 22 is numbered as scroll 13. This error may explain why the actual scroll 13 (the third part of chapter 7) was not carved.

The engraved chapter deals with some of the basic doctrines of the Nirvana Sutra, such as the eternity of the Dharma body (fashen changzhu 法身常住), and the four positive attributes of nirvana: Permanence (chang 常), Bliss (le 樂), Self (wo 我), and Purity (jing 淨).“Central to the text is the notion that the Buddha’s last earthly life as Śākyamuni was merely a docetistic show for the benefit of sentient beings; in fact, he entered into mahānirvāṇa countless eons ago.” . This chapter, thus can be regarded as presenting the core of the entire sutra.

On wall e, the engraving does not, as one would expect, continue from the corner with wall d. Only one text is engraved here, which may indicate another change in plan. The entire western part of wall e is untouched. On its eastern half, a field of script with 77 columns is marked by vertical grid lines; once more, here is a new text, the Sutra of Liberation and Breaking the Attributes of the Mind through the Wisdom Stored in the Ocean of Buddha-nature (Foxinghai zang zhihui jietuo po xinxiang jing 佛性海藏智慧解脫破心相經; hereafter: Sutra on the Wisdom Stored in the Ocean of Buddha-nature). Again, only a part of the entire text is engraved, the first of its two scrolls. Yet the vertical grid lines continue for more than 40 cm beyond the last column, clearly indicating the intention to engrave the second scroll. There was plenty of space; the rest of the entire wall was available, but it was not used. Rather surprisingly, the 77 columns of the extant engraving are read from left to right. This is one of only four instances in the precinct, where the sequence of the columns is reversed. The other three cases are seen in the neighboring cave 51.

The apocryphal scripture, like several others, purports to have been spoken by the Buddha shortly before he entered nirvana. The pivotal term in the text is Ocean of Buddha-nature (foxinghai 佛性海). In commentaries to the Nirvana Sutra it is equated with the Ocean of Great Nirvana. Thus, this sutra, too, is squarely bound in with the overarching theme of the Grove. Chen Frederick Shih-Chung considers it an apocryphal, condensed paraphrase of the long Nirvana Sutra. Specifically, he points out that key metaphors, the Poisonous Arrows, the Four Poisonous Serpents, the Five Aggregates, and the Six Great Robbers are present in both texts.See the article “The Sutra on the Wisdom Stored in the Ocean of Buddha-nature” by Chen Frederick Shih-Chung in the present volume. Apart from the Poisonous Arrows, these metaphors occur in the Nirvana Sutra in its scroll 23, which was never carved. Claudia Wenzel has, therefore, suggested that the Sutra on the Wisdom Stored in the Ocean of Buddha-nature was understood as a summary that could replace the entire latter part of the Nirvana Sutra, once the monks had realized that this long text might not be completed at the Grove. The engravers of the Sutra on the Wisdom Stored in the Ocean of Buddha-nature may have reversed the sequence of the columns, to stave off further endeavors in engraving the Nirvana Sutra.Personal communication, April 28, 2015.

None of the four texts surveyed so far on walls d and e are complete. The shortfall may be symptomatic of a situation, in which competing decisions were made about the texts that should be engraved in this cave. Yet, possibly only after the issue of the Nirvana Sutra had been settled, four, albeit shorter, texts were engraved on wall f, three of them in their entirety. They display different layouts and show the hands of different calligraphers.

The first of the four texts is the Diamond Perfection of Wisdom Sutra ( Vajracchedikā-prajñā­pāra­mitā-sūtra ; Jin’gang bore boluomi jing 金剛般若波羅蜜經; hereafter: Diamond Sutra) in 51 columns.On this text in the Grove see
68–78. In the kaiyuan era (713–741) this foundational text of Mahayana Buddhism was perhaps regarded as the most representative of all Buddhist sutras. When Emperor Xuanzong 玄宗 (r. 713–756) wrote commentaries on Confucian, Daoist, and Buddhist scriptures, “jing 經”, he chose, in 722, the Confucian Classic of Filial Piety (Xiaojing 孝經), in 732, the Daoist Scripture of the Virtue and its Power (Daodejing
道德經) and, in 735, the Buddhist Diamond Sutra.

As in the case of the same sutra engraved on wall f of cave 33, the calligraphy of this famous text is very neat. In cave 33 the characters are arranged in a notional grid of circa 2 cm; here, in cave 46, even, actual thin vertical and horizontal grid lines form squares of 2.5 x 2.5 cm. A third engraving of the Diamond Sutra at the Grove is found on wall d in cave 73 in section E. There, the celebrated text is also written within neat grid lines, with squares of 2.1 x 2.1 cm.

The next three texts on wall f of cave 46 follow consecutively without their starting point being marked by a new column; they are also not so neatly carved and lack horizontal rows. Their calligraphic styles all differ; they were written by different persons.

First, following the Diamond Sutra, in the tighter columns 51–71, is the complete text of the Sutra of the Glorious Buddha Crown Dhāraṇī (Foding zunsheng tuoluoni jing 佛頂尊勝陀羅尼經; hereafter: Sutra of the Buddha Crown). The first column states that Buddhapālita (Fotuopoli 佛陀波利; fl. 676–683), from the Kingdom of Jibin, presented this translation; it included the transliteration of the dhāraṇī formula in 35 sections. 罽賓國沙門佛陀波利奉 詔譯. See 2: 658–706: “Jibin and China as seen from Chinese Documents”. Pronunciations are indicated in small characters. These are arranged vertically in two half columns, which together fill the width of one column. Various recensions exist, with different numbers of sections, also transliterating the Sanskrit sounds with different characters. The transliterations by Buddhapālita offered here differ from those in the Taishō .

The Sutra of the Buddha Crown is one of the best-known and widely disseminated texts in East Asian Buddhism. In an edict of 776, Emperor Daizong 代宗 (762–779) ordered its daily recitation by all clerics throughout the empire. The sutra tells a story of a god (deva; tian 天) who hears a prophecy that after seven days he will meet an unfortunate rebirth. Thereupon, on his behalf, Indra turns to the Buddha for help. The Buddha advises him to recite the dhāraṇī twenty-one times every day. After six days and nights the god’s life is prolonged and calamity averted.

Thereafter follows, in columns 71–84, the already mentioned Perfection of Wisdom in the Sutras, again without observing horizontal rows. This text from the Perfection of Wisdom literature was composed in China, probably in the fifth or sixth century. It was never included in the canon and only fragments are found in Dunhuang manuscripts. The text at the Grove is the only complete version known to date. The sutra, at least in its title, purports to be a summary of a larger text corpus, and in this respect is comparable with the Sutra on the Wisdom Stored in the Ocean of Buddha-nature. The reason for its placement in the Grove is the Buddha’s announcement that he will soon enter nirvana.

The fourth and last text on this wall is the Sutra Spoken by the Buddha on Amitābha (Fo shuo Amituo jing 佛說阿彌陀經; hereafter: Amitābha Sutra) in columns 84–91. These columns are again well spaced, but not as those of the Diamond Sutra. The last columns are damaged.

The Amitābha Sutra is one of the three foundational texts of the Pure Land School in East Asian Buddhism.For this text see the article “The Amitābha Sutra” by Claudia Wenzel in the present volume. The most remarkable feature of the engraving at the Grove is that it is the earliest known instance of a variant tradition, described as a Popular Recension. In this version of the text, devotees are assured, that the mere hearing of the names of Buddhas and of the sutra title alone opens up the path to the Pure Land, while the canonical tradition requires hearing, upholding, and even memorizing the sutra. When Hōnen (法然; 1133–1212) established the Pure Land School in Japan this difference was crucial. It certainly was one of the main reasons for Hōnen’s success that he, based on the Popular Recension, foregrounded faith rather than self-cultivation in a person’s quest for attaining rebirth in the Pure Land. In cave 46, the devout may have been able to make a connection to the sentence quoted above from the Perfection of Wisdom in the Sutras: “Wherever there exists the title of this sutra, there also is the Buddha.”See above, note .

Two short dhāraṇī
spells are engraved on wall h, the eastern soffit of the door opening. The first is the Spell of the Glorious Buddha Crown Dhāraṇī (Foding zunsheng tuoluoni zhou 佛頂尊勝陀羅尼咒; hereafter: Spell of the Buddha Crown). After its last character, in the twelfth column, follow the date of 733 and the signature of Monk Ruyi 如一. Only parts of the text were originally engraved. In the portions left empty, many characters were later added, somewhat arbitrarily. Nevertheless, a full reconstruction has been achieved (fig. ).Reconstruction by Tsai Suey-ling.
This recension of the Spell of the Buddha Crown, as stated in its first column, is again ascribed to Buddhapālita, who also translated and transcribed the Sutra of the Buddha Crown on wall f. Yet the difference between the two versions is remarkable. The same Sanskrit text is divided into 35 segments on wall f, while the version on wall h has 52 segments and also uses other transliterations. Obviously, Monk Ruyi was convinced, that he was engraving the accurate version.For a discussion see the article “The Sutra of the Glorious Buddha Crown Dhāraṇī ” by Manuel Sassmann and Tsai Suey-ling in the present volume.

The second dhāraṇī
text, the Spell Spoken by Buddha Amitābha (Amituo fo shuo zhou
阿彌陀佛說咒) ends in column 16 and is followed by the signature of Monk Xuanying. This text, too, has many empty spaces, but, again, a reconstruction was possible. The text can be considered an addition to the Amitābha Sutra carved on wall f. The spell reinforces the message of the Amitābha Sutra, especially that of its Popular Recension, namely, that rebirth in the Pure Land can be achieved just by invocation: “If you recite it [the spell] three hundred thousand times, then you will meet Buddha Amitābha face-to-face, and will be destined to achieve rebirth in the Pure Land of Bliss.” See the article by Tsai Suey-Ling and Claudia Wenzel, “The Spell Spoken by Buddha Amitābha” in the present volume, .

It was clearly intentional that the two dhāraṇī texts on wall h were placed near the entrance. Here is the space between the outside world and the inner sanctuary of the cave, where the faithful are shown the path to salvation. In the same space, on wall i, the Medicine Buddha offers consolation with his Life Prolonging Banner, and on the opposite side of the cave’s entrance, a benevolent bodhisattva dispenses his gifts. This liminal space must also have been an area, where rituals made its salvific potentials become reality.See also the article by Martin Bemmann “The Banner Carved on Wall i of Cave 46” in the present volume.

Looking at all texts in cave 46, it appears that they form a coherent group. The incomplete texts on walls d and e have something in common: They represent, pars pro toto, much larger texts. Every title in the Catalog of All Canonical Scriptures stands for an entire sutra; the seven large characters in column 69 invoke the essence of the Prajñāpāramitā literature; scroll 22 (numbered scroll 13) from the Nirvana Sutra contains some of the core doctrines from this colossal text, and the Sutra on the Wisdom Stored in the Ocean of Buddha-nature some of its key metaphors. The Perfection of Wisdom in the Sutras on wall f equally purports to offer a summary of the Perfection of Wisdom as it is taught in other sutras.

The other two texts on wall f that follow the magisterial Diamond Sutra, namely the Sutra of the Buddha Crown and the Amitābha Sutra, also have something in common: each, in their own way, holds out the promise that salvation can be reached by simple means. The faithful must merely recite formulaic spells in a prescribed time and frequence, and hear the names of the Buddhas as well as the sutra title. This assures rebirth in paradise. The two dhāraṇī spells at the entrance reinforce these promises and open the way to their realization.

The two groups of texts in cave 46, the ones that stand as a microcosm of much larger texts, and the texts that hold out promises of salvation, in turn also have something in common: They simplify an enormously complex and intricate belief system. The Buddhist adept who is overawed by a scriptural macrocosm, with millions of characters, learns here that the essence of the teachings can be condensed into a few short texts. And the believer who may despair at the incredibly arduous and convoluted path to salvation and may fear that he will never be able to complete all the exercises, which the texts describe and demand, is here offered an easy shortcut: Recite and listen.

Whereas ten separate texts in cave 46 engender a host of interesting and intricate issues, and present a salvific program to the faithful, only one text, as mentioned before, is seen on wall f in the central cave 51 of section C. It is part 4 of chapter 7, which is scroll 14, in the Nirvana Sutra, entitled “Chapter on Sacred Practice” (“Shengxing pin” 聖行品). Its 49 columns are written from left to right. Wang Yanzhao’s two colophons of 959 and 961 on the same wall, are, perhaps out of respect, also written from left to right.
The third cave 58 is completely devoid of engravings. Only one field of script has been levelled on wall e in preparation for future work.

div.head: Chronology
div.p: The thorny issue of chronology has now to be addressed, although it will not be possible to resolve it satisfactorily. Three kinds of evidence can be considered, the architecture, the colophons, and the arrangement of the texts.

The Nirvana Sutra, the most important sutra at the Grove, starts with scroll 1 in cave 59 in section D. The position of this cave in relation to section C is therefore significant. The easternmost wall of section C, which is wall f in cave 58, does not cut into the mountain in a straight line. It bends slightly inward before reaching the door that was broken through to cave 59 (ground plan fig. and fig. ). The front part of wall f is oriented at 274˚ and the rear part at 314˚. If wall f had been continued in a straight line, it would have cut into the adjacent cave 59. This change of plan indicates that cave 59 was already there, when cave 58 was hewn out. Indeed, cave 59 may have been the earliest cave in the entire compound. The elaborate ornamental borders around the fields of script in this cave, which are the only examples in the Grove, are testimony of the high expectations with which the project started. Moreover, the cave faces the Buddha figure on the other side of the valley (its walls d and f have directions of 313˚ and 304˚, respectively).

On wall f of this cave 59 is a colophon dated 735. It was written by a donor, who wanted texts with instructions on meditation engraved, thereby bringing the work on the Nirvana Sutra scrolls in the cave to an end. The construction of the cave thus must have begun several years earlier.

On the western side of section C, on the front wall of the passageway between caves 46 and 51, is the early colophon of 723, twelve years earlier than the one in cave 59. Construction of section C must have been well underway, when this colophon was carved. If cave 59 is indeed the earliest cave at the Grove, work there must have begun some time before 723. It thus appears, that the three caves in section C and the adjacent cave 59 in section D were probably all available by 723.

After the first five scrolls of the Nirvana Sutra had been finished in cave 59, it would have made sense, as already suggested above, to fill the three oversize caves of section C with that sutra. Yet this is not what happened. Instead, the engravers turned to the west, to cave 66, then to cave 83.

One has to assume that other decision makers in the monastery had taken hold of cave 46 and begun to engrave texts there. However, the curtailed texts on wall d seem to indicate that their decisions did not go unchallenged. The carvers of the Nirvana Sutra at least managed to place one scroll on this wall, and also into the neighboring cave 51, but here their efforts petered out. The engraving of the Sutra on the Wisdom Stored in the Ocean of Buddha-nature on the rear wall of cave 46 may have signaled a compromise that the different parties could accept.

The disarray in section C may possibly be explained by the likelihood that different factions of monks were working simultaneously without overall coordination, in different directions, and in competition. These factions were based in the different courts (yuan 院) within the monastery, which the stele of 1103 mentions. The use of this term implies that various groups of monks coexisted at the Grove. Abbots must have had a hard time to align them.

Abbots also interacted with other monasteries in the vicinity, as shown by the inscriptions of the Presiding Monk Xuanying in locations other than the Grove. Monasteries competed for donors, for calligraphers, sculptors, and engravers. In this respect, the remote Grove may have been in a less favorable position than more urban centers.

A further complication may have been caused by the involvement of donors. They required texts that did not necessarily fit with the intentions of the monks, as witnessed by the colophon of 735 in cave 59. Other donors wanted to dedicate sculptures, as the writer of the colophon of 723. It may also have been a donor’s wish that in cave 73 a space prepared for engraving texts was filled instead with figures.

Finally, more general, political factors shaped the fate of the engraving project at the Grove. These included the catastrophe of the An Lushan 安祿山 rebellion of 756 and, one century later, the horrors of the Buddhist persecution of the huichang 會昌 era (841–846). Details of how these events affected work at the Grove are no longer known.
